providerExtensions.name: Hook event system
providerExtensions.description: Gemini CLI supports 11 hook events covering the full agent lifecycle: BeforeTool, AfterTool, BeforeAgent, AfterAgent, BeforeModel, AfterModel, BeforeToolSelection, SessionStart, SessionEnd, PreCompress, and Notification. Hooks are shell commands configured per-event in settings.json under the hooks key. Each event entry is an array of hook definition objects with optional matcher, optional sequential flag, and a hooks array of command configs.

providerExtensions.name: stdin/stdout JSON I/O protocol
providerExtensions.description: Hooks communicate via stdin (JSON input) and stdout (JSON output). All hooks receive a common base input with session_id, transcript_path, cwd, hook_event_name, and timestamp. Output fields include decision (allow/deny/block), reason, systemMessage, suppressOutput, continue, and stopReason. Stdout must contain only the JSON object — any plain text pollutes the parse. Stderr is safe for debug logging and is never parsed.

providerExtensions.name: Exit code semantics
providerExtensions.description: Exit code 0 means success — stdout is parsed as JSON. Exit code 2 means system block — the target action is aborted and stderr is used as the rejection reason. Any other exit code is a non-fatal warning — the interaction continues with original parameters. This three-tier exit code contract is specific to Gemini CLI's hook system.

providerExtensions.name: Matcher-based hook filtering
providerExtensions.description: Hook definitions support a matcher field to filter which tools or triggers fire the hook. For BeforeTool and AfterTool, matchers are regular expressions matched against the tool name (e.g., "write_.*"). For lifecycle events, matchers are exact strings (e.g., "startup"). An empty string or "*" matches all occurrences.

providerExtensions.name: Project hook fingerprinting
providerExtensions.description: Project-level hooks (.gemini/settings.json) are fingerprinted by Gemini CLI. If a hook's name or command changes (e.g., via git pull), it is treated as a new untrusted hook and the user is warned before execution. This is a security measure for untrusted project contexts.

providerExtensions.name: Sequential execution flag
providerExtensions.description: Hook definition groups support a sequential boolean field. When true, hooks in the group run one after another in order. When false (or omitted), hooks run in parallel. This allows dependency ordering within a hook event group.

providerExtensions.name: Event-specific hook output fields
providerExtensions.description: Several events support hookSpecificOutput fields beyond the common output schema: BeforeTool supports tool_input (merges with and overrides model arguments); AfterTool supports additionalContext (appended to tool result) and tailToolCallRequest (routes execution to another tool, replacing the original response); AfterAgent supports clearContext (clears LLM conversation history while preserving UI display); BeforeModel supports llm_request (overrides outgoing request) and llm_response (provides synthetic response, skipping the LLM call entirely); BeforeToolSelection supports toolConfig with mode (AUTO/ANY/NONE) and allowedFunctionNames.

providerExtensions.name: In-session hook management commands
providerExtensions.description: Hooks can be managed interactively without editing JSON: /hooks panel displays all configured hooks; /hooks enable-all and /hooks disable-all toggle the entire hook system; /hooks enable <name> and /hooks disable <name> toggle individual hooks by name. Changes take effect immediately within the session.
